section.title: Offerings for the Appointed Feasts
section.sectionRationale: Continuing the offering calendar begun in chapter 28, this chapter completes the year with the great autumn feasts: the Feast of Trumpets, the Day of Atonement, and the seven, then eight, days of the Feast of Tabernacles. Together these close out Israel's worship calendar just before the record turns to vows, war, and the settlement of the land.
section.thesis: The LORD prescribes offerings for the Feast of Trumpets, the solemn day of humbling on the tenth of the seventh month, and the extended Feast of Tabernacles, with its decreasing bulls each day and a closing eighth-day assembly, completing the yearly rhythm of worship that anchors Israel's national life to God.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the Feast of Trumpets on the first day of the seventh month; the solemn day of humbling and atonement on the tenth day; and the seven days of the Feast of Tabernacles with its closing eighth-day assembly.

units.label: The Feast of Trumpets
units.note: a day to sound the trumpets

verses.text: “On the first day of the seventh month, you are to hold a sacred assembly, and you must not do any regular work. This will be a day for you to sound the {{trumpets|trumpets}}.
As a pleasing aroma to the LORD, you are to present a burnt offering of one young bull, one ram, and seven male lambs a year old, all unblemished,
together with their grain offerings of fine flour mixed with oil, three-tenths of an ephah with the bull, two-tenths of an ephah with the ram,
and a tenth of an ephah with each of the seven male lambs.
Include one male goat as a sin offering to make atonement for you.
These are in addition to the monthly and daily burnt offerings with their prescribed grain offerings and drink offerings. They are a pleasing aroma, an offering made by fire to the LORD.

units.label: The tenth day: humbling and atonement

media.alt: A solitary altar under a still sky on the tenth day of the seventh month
media.caption: A sacred assembly, a day to humble the soul before the LORD.

verses.text: On the tenth day of this seventh month, you are to hold a sacred assembly, and you shall {{humble|humble yourselves}}; you must not do any work.
Present as a pleasing aroma to the LORD a burnt offering of one young bull, one ram, and seven male lambs a year old, all unblemished,
together with their grain offerings of fine flour mixed with oil, three-tenths of an ephah with the bull, two-tenths of an ephah with the ram,
and a tenth of an ephah with each of the seven lambs.
Include one male goat for a sin offering, in addition to the sin offering of atonement and the regular burnt offering with its grain offering and drink offerings.

units.label: The Feast of Tabernacles begins
units.note: a feast to the LORD for seven days

verses.text: On the fifteenth day of the seventh month, you are to hold a sacred assembly; you must not do any regular work, and you shall observe a feast to the LORD for seven days.
As a pleasing aroma to the LORD, you are to present an offering made by fire, a burnt offering of thirteen young bulls, two rams, and fourteen male lambs a year old, all unblemished,
along with the grain offering of three-tenths of an ephah of fine flour mixed with oil with each of the thirteen bulls, two-tenths of an ephah with each of the two rams,
and a tenth of an ephah with each of the fourteen lambs.
Include one male goat as a sin offering, in addition to the regular burnt offering with its grain offering and drink offering.

units.label: The seven days of the feast

verses.text: On the second day you are to present twelve young bulls, two rams, and fourteen male lambs a year old, all unblemished,
along with the grain and drink offerings for the bulls, rams, and lambs, according to the number prescribed.
Include one male goat as a sin offering, in addition to the regular burnt offering with its grain offering and drink offering.
On the third day you are to present eleven bulls, two rams, and fourteen male lambs a year old, all unblemished,
along with the grain and drink offerings for the bulls, rams, and lambs, according to the number prescribed.
Include one male goat as a sin offering, in addition to the regular burnt offering with its grain offering and drink offering.
On the fourth day you are to present ten bulls, two rams, and fourteen male lambs a year old, all unblemished,
along with the grain and drink offerings for the bulls, rams, and lambs, according to the number prescribed.
Include one male goat as a sin offering, in addition to the regular burnt offering with its grain offering and drink offering.
On the fifth day you are to present nine bulls, two rams, and fourteen male lambs a year old, all unblemished,
along with the grain and drink offerings for the bulls, rams, and lambs, according to the number prescribed.
Include one male goat as a sin offering, in addition to the regular burnt offering with its grain offering and drink offering.
On the sixth day you are to present eight bulls, two rams, and fourteen male lambs a year old, all unblemished,
along with the grain and drink offerings for the bulls, rams, and lambs, according to the number prescribed.
Include one male goat as a sin offering, in addition to the regular burnt offering with its grain offering and drink offering.
On the seventh day you are to present seven bulls, two rams, and fourteen male lambs a year old, all unblemished,
along with the grain and drink offerings for the bulls, rams, and lambs, according to the number prescribed.
Include one male goat as a sin offering, in addition to the regular burnt offering with its grain offering and drink offering.

units.label: The eighth day and closing summary
units.note: just as the LORD had commanded

verses.text: On the eighth day you are to hold a solemn assembly; you must not do any regular work.
As a pleasing aroma to the LORD, you are to present an offering made by fire, a burnt offering of one bull, one ram, and seven male lambs a year old, all unblemished,
along with the grain and drink offerings for the bulls, rams, and lambs, according to the number prescribed.
Include one male goat as a sin offering, in addition to the regular burnt offering with its grain offering and drink offering.
You are to present these offerings to the LORD at your appointed times, in addition to your vow and freewill offerings, whether burnt offerings, grain offerings, drink offerings, or peace offerings.”
So Moses spoke all this to the Israelites just as the LORD had commanded him.

trumpets.gloss: A shout or trumpet blast, an alarm. The Feast of Trumpets marked the start of the seventh month with a sound meant to gather Israel's attention and call the nation to renewed remembrance before God, later becoming the basis for the Jewish new year, Rosh Hashanah.

humble.gloss: To humble, to afflict oneself. This tenth-day command to humble the soul, understood in Leviticus 16 and 23 as fasting and repentance, marks this as Israel's most solemn day, the Day of Atonement, when the nation's sins were symbolically covered before the LORD.

basic.context: This chapter completes the yearly worship calendar begun in the previous one, covering the autumn feasts: a trumpet blast calling Israel to attention on the first of the seventh month, a solemn day of humbling and atonement on the tenth, and an extended, joyful seven-day (then eight-day) Feast of Tabernacles closing out the sacred year.
basic.hermeneutics: This is detailed ceremonial law establishing Israel's national religious calendar, not primarily moral instruction for individual readers. Its purpose is to structure an entire year of corporate worship around remembrance, repentance, and celebration before God.
basic.originalLanguages: The teruah, trumpet blast, opens the seventh month with an alarm calling Israel to attention. Ten days later comes the day Israel must anah, humble or afflict, themselves, a solemn day of national repentance later called the Day of Atonement, before the year closes with the joyful, decreasing-sacrifice rhythm of the Feast of Tabernacles.
basic.typology: The steadily decreasing number of bulls across the seven days of Tabernacles, from thirteen down to seven, some later Jewish tradition read as representing the nations of the world, gradually diminishing, while Israel's own eighth-day assembly stood alone and complete, a picture some Christian readers connect to the ingathering of the nations ultimately fulfilled in Christ's kingdom.
basic.christ: The Day of Atonement's sin offering, repeated here as throughout Israel's worship, anticipates the once-for-all atonement Christ accomplishes; Hebrews contrasts the yearly repetition of such offerings with Christ's single sacrifice, which need never be repeated because it fully and finally deals with sin, Hebrews 9:25-26.
basic.god: God structures even Israel's most joyful national festival, Tabernacles, around continued sin offerings, showing that celebration before Him never bypasses the reality of sin; yet He also ordains genuine, extended joy, seven full days of feasting, showing that atonement and celebration belong together rather than in opposition.
basic.crossReferences: Leviticus 16 and 23 supply the fuller instructions for the Day of Atonement and Feast of Tabernacles that this chapter assumes. Hebrews 9:25-26 contrasts the yearly repeated Day of Atonement offering with Christ's single, sufficient sacrifice. John 7:37-38 has Jesus speaking on the last, greatest day of this very feast, offering Himself as the living water it anticipated.
basic.oneStory: Israel's sacred year moves from alarm, to humbling, to joyful celebration, a rhythm of repentance and gladness held together rather than separated. That same rhythm finds its full and final meaning in Christ, whose once-for-all atonement fulfills the Day of Atonement's endless repetition, and whose living water answers the very feast John records Him addressing directly.

context.literary: This chapter completes the offering calendar begun in Numbers 28, moving from daily and weekly rhythms there to the great annual, seventh-month climax here, closing Israel's sacred year with its most solemn and its most joyful observances back to back.
context.historical: The seventh month, later called Tishrei, became the anchor of the Jewish civil and religious calendar, containing the new year, the holiest day of the year, and the great harvest festival, all within roughly three weeks, a rhythm still observed in Judaism today.
context.canonical: This chapter's Day of Atonement offering became the template for Israel's yearly national atonement ritual detailed fully in Leviticus 16, while the Feast of Tabernacles later became the occasion for some of Jesus' most significant public teaching, recorded in John 7 to 8.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To establish the specific offerings required for Israel's most solemn and most celebratory annual observances, ensuring the nation's worship calendar remained complete and properly ordered once settled in the land.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This chapter is ceremonial law tied to Israel's tabernacle and later temple system; it does not prescribe animal sacrifice or a specific liturgical calendar for the church today. Its abiding principle, that repentance and celebration both have their proper place before God, carries forward into Christian worship in a form fulfilled by Christ rather than repeated in these rituals.

debates.question: Why does the number of bulls offered decrease by one each day across the seven days of Tabernacles?

views.label: A practical, deliberate pattern with symbolic completeness
views.case: The total across all seven days, seventy bulls, matches the traditional number of the nations listed in Genesis 10, leading some Jewish and Christian interpreters to read the decreasing offerings as representing intercession for all the nations of the world during this feast.

views.label: No fixed symbolic meaning is stated in the text itself
views.case: The text gives no explicit reason for the decreasing count, and some interpreters caution against over-reading numerical patterns the passage itself does not explain, treating the decrease simply as the prescribed ritual detail.

debates.bearing: Whether or not the specific numerical symbolism holds, the passage's larger point stands regardless: God ordained an extensive, costly, and complete pattern of offerings marking Israel's most joyful festival, underscoring that even celebration remained tied to atonement and to God's own instruction rather than spontaneous human invention.

study.originalLanguages: The teruah, trumpet blast, opening the seventh month calls Israel to alert attention before God. Ten days later, the command to anah, humble or afflict the soul, marks Israel's most solemn national day of repentance, later formalized as Yom Kippur, before the sacred year closes with the extended joy of Tabernacles, each day's offering still paired with a sin offering for atonement.

typology.type: The Day of Atonement's yearly repeated sin offering
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ's single, sufficient sacrifice, which unlike this yearly repetition never needs to be offered again, Hebrews 9:25-26.

typology.type: The Feast of Tabernacles and its climactic final day
typology.fulfillment: Jesus stands on the last, greatest day of this very feast and offers Himself as the source of living water, John 7:37-38, fulfilling the feast's deepest longing.

study.christ: Hebrews 9:25-26 draws directly on the yearly repetition of the Day of Atonement offering described here: the high priest enters the Most Holy Place every year with blood not his own, but Christ has appeared once for all at the culmination of the ages to put away sin by the sacrifice of Himself. John 7:37-38 records Jesus standing during the very Feast of Tabernacles this chapter prescribes, crying out on its last and greatest day that anyone who thirsts should come to Him and drink, fulfilling the feast's themes of provision and ingathering in His own person.
study.god: God ordains both solemn repentance and extended celebration within the very same sacred month, refusing to separate the sober reality of sin from the genuine joy of festival gladness. His holiness requires atonement even during Israel's most joyous week of the year, yet His generosity provides for seven, then eight, full days of feasting before Him.

crossReferences.note: Supplies the fuller ritual instructions for the Day of Atonement that this chapter's offerings assume.
Gives the fuller instructions and meaning for the Feast of Tabernacles referenced in this chapter.
Contrasts the yearly repeated Day of Atonement offering with Christ's single, sufficient sacrifice for sin.
Jesus offers Himself as living water on the last, greatest day of the Feast of Tabernacles this chapter prescribes.

study.oneStory: Israel's sacred year moves through alarm, humbling, and joyful celebration, atonement and gladness held together rather than kept apart. That same movement finds its final, complete answer in Christ, whose single sacrifice fulfills the endlessly repeated Day of Atonement, and whose invitation to living water answers the deepest hope of the very feast this chapter prescribes.

quiz.q: What marked the first day of the seventh month, according to verse 1?

options.text: A sacred assembly and a day for sounding the trumpets.
options.note: Verse 1 states this exactly.

options.text: A day of complete fasting and silence.
options.note: That description fits the tenth day of humbling, not the trumpet day on the first.

options.text: The beginning of the seven-day Feast of Tabernacles.
options.note: Tabernacles begins on the fifteenth day, not the first, according to verse 12.

options.text: No specific observance is described for this day.
options.note: Verse 1 describes it explicitly.

quiz.q: What were the Israelites commanded to do on the tenth day of the seventh month, according to verse 7?

options.text: Hold a sacred assembly, humble themselves, and do no work.
options.note: Verse 7 states this exactly, describing what became known as the Day of Atonement.

options.text: Celebrate with feasting for a full week.
options.note: That describes the Feast of Tabernacles beginning on the fifteenth day, not this solemn tenth-day observance.

options.text: Offer the annual census of the people.
options.note: No census is connected to this day; it is described as a day of humbling and sacred assembly.

options.text: No specific instruction is given for this day.
options.note: Verse 7 gives clear instructions.

quiz.q: How did the number of bulls offered change across the seven days of the Feast of Tabernacles?

options.text: It decreased by one each day, from thirteen down to seven.
options.note: Verses 13, 17, 20, 23, 26, 29, and 32 show this exact decreasing pattern.

options.text: It remained the same number every day.
options.note: The text shows a clear decreasing pattern, not a constant number.

options.text: It increased by one each day.
options.note: The pattern moves in the opposite direction, decreasing rather than increasing.

options.text: No specific numbers are given for each day.
options.note: Each day's specific bull count is given in detail.

quiz.q: What happened on the eighth day, immediately following the seven days of Tabernacles?

options.text: A solemn assembly was held, with no regular work and a distinct, smaller set of offerings.
options.note: Verses 35 to 38 describe this closing eighth day separately from the preceding seven.

options.text: The feast simply continued exactly as the seventh day had.
options.note: The eighth day is marked as a distinct solemn assembly with its own smaller offering, one bull rather than seven.

options.text: All offerings ceased and the people returned home immediately.
options.note: A specific, though smaller, set of offerings is still prescribed for this eighth day.

options.text: No eighth day observance is mentioned.
options.note: Verses 35 to 38 describe it in detail.

quiz.q: According to verse 39, how do these prescribed offerings relate to vow and freewill offerings?

options.text: These appointed offerings are in addition to whatever vow and freewill offerings the people bring.
options.note: Verse 39 states this relationship directly.

options.text: Vow and freewill offerings replace these appointed offerings entirely.
options.note: The text says these offerings stand in addition to, not as a replacement for, personal vow and freewill offerings.

options.text: Vow and freewill offerings were forbidden during these appointed feasts.
options.note: No such prohibition is stated; the text explicitly allows for both types together.

options.text: No relationship between the two types of offerings is mentioned.
options.note: Verse 39 directly addresses this relationship.

questions: The trumpet blast called Israel to attention at the start of the sacred month. What in your own life currently serves as a wake-up call to renewed attention toward God?
The tenth day required Israel to humble themselves honestly before God. Where do you need to practice that kind of honest humbling rather than presenting a polished version of yourself to Him?
The Feast of Tabernacles combined real joy with ongoing atonement offerings every single day. Where might you need to hold celebration and repentance together rather than treating them as opposites?
Jesus stood on the last day of this very feast and offered Himself as living water to anyone who thirsts. What are you currently thirsting for that only He can truly satisfy?

This whole sacred calendar had to be repeated every single year, never finally finishing the work of atonement. How does resting in Christ's one, final sacrifice change the way you approach your own sense of needing to atone for yourself?

translationNotes.note: This chapter never names the tenth day as Yom Kippur, the Day of Atonement; that name and its fuller ritual, including the scapegoat, comes from Leviticus 16 and 23, which this passage assumes its readers already know.

The Feast of Tabernacles, Sukkot, involved living in temporary shelters for seven days, commemorating Israel's wilderness journey, Leviticus 23:42-43, a detail not repeated in this chapter's purely sacrificial instructions.

The eighth day, described as a solemn assembly distinct from the preceding seven, is called Shemini Atzeret in later Jewish tradition and is treated in the Hebrew text as its own separate, smaller festival rather than simply the feast's final day.
